Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • 01Explain the difference between data and spatial data.
  • 02Explain the difference between spatial data and spatial information.
  • 03Define geographic information systems.
  • 04Define geographic information science.
  • 05Describe the components of a GIS.
  • 06Explain why GIS matters.
Brainstorm together
Is it spatial?

For each one, take a class vote first. Tap a button, debate it, then reveal the reasoning.

Spatial data has both attributes and coordinate locations.
It answers the question: “where” is “what”?
Brainstorm together
What is one example of spatial data on campus?

For each example, push on the “where”: what exact point would the coordinates reference? The trunk of a tree? The wall of a building? The center of a path?

ExampleWhere do the coordinates reference?AttributesInformation it could give

Cells are editable — click and type. Clears on refresh.

From data to information

Spatial information

Spatial information is data that has been analyzed in some way to reveal patterns and relationships.

Collection gives you data. Analysis is what turns it into information — something useful for a decision.

Brainstorm together
What information could we get from our campus spatial data?

Look back at the table you just filled in — what patterns or answers could analysis pull out of it?

Brainstorm together
How do we go from data to information?
The tool The tool we use to transform spatial data into information is called a Geographic Information System (GIS).
Brainstorm together
How do we define a Geographic Information System?

Build a class definition first. Capture the keywords students offer, then compare.

Our class definition
GIS A computer-based system to aid in the collection, maintenance, storage, analysis, output, and distribution of spatial information.
Brainstorm together
What are the components of a GIS?

Fill the definition and a class example for each before revealing the reference answers.

ComponentDefinitionExample from our class
Spatial Data
Software
Hardware
Methods
People
Brainstorm together
How do we define Geographic Information Science?
GIScience The science behind the systems: using a GIS to conduct scientific research, and improving or inventing the GIS software, data, and methods themselves — along with studying what those tools mean and where they fall short.
GIScience · origins
  • Coined by Michael F. Goodchild at a few keynote presentations and later published in 1992.
  • Challenged the academic community to move from a focus on the technical toward the intellectual challenges and scientific questions posed by using GIS.
  • Ensured the long-term survival of GIS as a field.
Brainstorm together · from the reading
What is the difference between GIS and GIScience?

Collect contrasts in your own words first.

GIS — the tool/systemGIScience — the science of the tool
One way to frame it GIS is the software/system you operate to collect, store, analyze, and map spatial data. GIScience is the research and method-building behind it — asking what the tools mean, where they fall short, and inventing better ones. Using ArcGIS Pro to map clinic access is GIS; designing & publishing a new travel-time accessibility method is GIScience.
Breakout activity · pairs

Why do GIS and GIScience matter?

Instructions: Choose one example of GIS in action — navigation apps, food delivery, weather maps, disaster response, public health, real estate, ride-sharing, utilities, and so on.

15:00

Need inspiration?

Explore a story map, search Google Scholar for “GIS Applications,” or open a live dashboard:

2024 ArcGIS StoryMaps winnersstorymaps.arcgis.com USGS Latest Earthquakesearthquake.usgs.gov NASA FIRMS Fire Mapfirms.modaps.eosdis.nasa.gov AirNow Fire & Smokefire.airnow.gov Global Disaster Alerts (GDACS)gdacs.org Global Forest Watchglobalforestwatch.org LA Equity Explorerexperience.arcgis.com Tree Inventory Dashboardarcgis.com

Write one short paragraph (5–7 sentences) with your partner

  • ?The problem: What need or question is being addressed?
  • ?Spatial data used: GPS points, addresses, roads, parcels, satellite imagery, census areas…
  • ?Information produced: a map, route, risk score, hotspot map, service area, suitability map…
  • ?Challenges / limitations: data collection, accuracy, privacy, updating, analysis choices, bias, uncertainty, communication.

Deliverable: post your paragraph, the link/source you used, and a figure from the source to the Canvas Discussion Board. You’ll have 15–20 minutes to research and write before we share as a class.

Worked example

Integrating human mobility and animal movement data — white-tailed deer in urban environments

A study set out to understand what kinds of landscapes white-tailed deer spend time in. GIS collects and stores movement data from deer fitted with GPS collars (Figure 1), plus landscape data (human activity, temperature, road density). GIS is then used to model the relationship between deer movement and those landscape variables. The study finds deer spend more time in landscapes with high human activity, especially residential areas (Figure 2). One challenge: data is limited to where deer were captured (urban park areas), which could bias the results.

Key takeaways

1
Spatial data tells us “where is what.”
2
Spatial information comes from analysis, not collection.
3
A GIS is the tool/system that converts spatial data into spatial information.
4
GIScience is the “science of GIS” — the intellectual and methodological work behind it, and what it means in practice.

Key terms & concepts

Open any term to see its definition and an example.
